children.content:
At the 2023 World Economic Forum in Davos, Intel CEO
Pat Gelsinger said “there is no innovation without
semiconductors”. As the global economy becomes
increasingly dependent on digital technology, he
argued that “the world needs more geographically
balanced and resilient chip supply chains” 13.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><LEFT>At the 2023 World Economic Forum in Davos, Intel CEO</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>Pat Gelsinger said “there is no innovation without</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>semiconductors”. As the global economy becomes</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>increasingly dependent on digital technology, he</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>argued that “the world needs more geographically</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>balanced and resilient chip supply chains” 13.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
This is because many computer chip companies
(including NVIDIA) are ‘fabless’ organisations, meaning
they design and sell chips, but do not manufacture
them. Their manufacturing is outsourced to a
specialised ‘semiconductor foundry’, such as  TSMC
(Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company)
which is by far the largest chip manufacturer in the
world with approximately 55% market share. The
second largest is Samsung in South Korea, with
approximately 15% market share in 2023.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><LEFT>This is because many computer chip companies</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>(including NVIDIA) are ‘fabless’ organisations, meaning</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>they design and sell chips, but do not manufacture</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>them. Their manufacturing is outsourced to a</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>specialised ‘semiconductor foundry’, such as  TSMC</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>(Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company)</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>which is by far the largest chip manufacturer in the</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>world with approximately 55% market share. The</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>second largest is Samsung in South Korea, with</LEFT></LINE><LINE><LEFT>approximately 15% market share in 2023.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content: Example chip producers (not an exhaustive list)

This means around 70% of the world’s computer chips are manufactured by just two organisations. These
power not only AI systems, but everything that requires a semiconductor chip, from cars to household
appliances. As the world emerged from the COVID-19 pandemic, this dependence was brought into sharp focus
with very public halts to car production lines due to a global chip shortage. Quite simply without TSMC the
global digital economy would grind to a halt. Raising awareness of this risk has led to further geo-political
tensions surrounding the future of Taiwan, where TSMC is based.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>This means around 70% of the world’s computer chips are manufactured by just two organisations. These</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>power not only AI systems, but everything that requires a semiconductor chip, from cars to household</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>appliances. As the world emerged from the COVID-19 pandemic, this dependence was brought into sharp focus</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>with very public halts to car production lines due to a global chip shortage. Quite simply without TSMC the</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>global digital economy would grind to a halt. Raising awareness of this risk has led to further geo-political</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>tensions surrounding the future of Taiwan, where TSMC is based.</CENTER></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
The very limited number of GPU chip designers and manufacturers around the world has therefore led several
major technology companies to develop their own chips to power AI applications, including Amazon, Apple,
Google, IBM, Microsoft and Tesla. Chip designers and manufacturers including Intel, AMD and Qualcomm are
also building new production facilities. TSMC, for example, is building a plant in Arizona (its first production
facility outside of Taiwan) and Intel is building a new plant in Ohio, with tax credits provided by the US Chips &
Science Act.
children.content_tagged: <TEXT><LINE><CENTER>The very limited number of GPU chip designers and manufacturers around the world has therefore led several</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>major technology companies to develop their own chips to power AI applications, including Amazon, Apple,</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>Google, IBM, Microsoft and Tesla. Chip designers and manufacturers including Intel, AMD and Qualcomm are</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>also building new production facilities. TSMC, for example, is building a plant in Arizona (its first production</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>facility outside of Taiwan) and Intel is building a new plant in Ohio, with tax credits provided by the US Chips &</CENTER></LINE><LINE><LEFT>Science Act.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>

children.content:
Since 2016, Google has also developed its own chip that is specifically designed for AI, called a TPU (Tensor
Processing Unit). Although GPU chips are at the core of most AI computing power today and can perform
tasks in parallel, a TPU chip takes this a step further and can perform ‘matrix calculations’ which significantly
accelerates machine learning tasks. These differences could be thought of like a traditional newspaper printing
press – a normal laptop computer could print one letter at a time, a GPU chip could print a whole line of text
at a time, and a TPU chip could print an entire page at a time.

Although GPU chips are at the core of most AI computing power today and can perform</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>tasks in parallel, a TPU chip takes this a step further and can perform ‘matrix calculations’ which significantly</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>accelerates machine learning tasks. These differences could be thought of like a traditional newspaper printing</CENTER></LINE><LINE><CENTER>press – a normal laptop computer could print one letter at a time, a GPU chip could print a whole line of text</CENTER></LINE><LINE><LEFT>at a time, and a TPU chip could print an entire page at a time.</LEFT></LINE></TEXT>
